g.data-narration: Welcome to Computer System Organization. We'll explore how hardware components work together to execute programs and process data efficiently.
A computer system has four main structural components: CPU, Main Memory, I/O Devices, and System Bus. These work together to process data.
The System Bus connects all components. Data flows between CPU, Memory, and I/O devices through this shared communication pathway.
The CPU has three main parts: Control Unit coordinates activities, ALU performs math and logic operations, and Registers provide fast storage.
The CPU operates in a cycle: Fetch instruction from memory, Decode what it means, Execute the operation, then Update the program counter.
Computer memory is organized in a hierarchy. Fastest is registers, then cache, RAM, ROM, and finally secondary storage. Each level trades speed for capacity.
Each memory type has different characteristics: Registers are fastest but smallest, cache is fast but limited, RAM is moderate, and secondary storage is slow but holds terabytes.
I/O devices communicate with CPU through three methods: Programmed I/O where CPU controls everything, Interrupt-driven I/O where devices signal when ready, and DMA for direct transfers.
Interrupts are signals requiring immediate attention. Types include hardware interrupts from devices, software interrupts from programs, and traps for error conditions.
When an interrupt occurs: CPU finishes current instruction, saves the current state, executes the interrupt service routine, then restores state and continues.
Storage devices include magnetic disks with tracks and sectors, and SSDs with flash memory. HDDs use mechanical movement while SSDs have no moving parts.
g.text: Flash memory • No moving parts • Blocks/Pages

g.data-narration: When a computer starts, it goes through boot process: Power-on, POST test, Bootstrap loader loads OS kernel, system initializes, then user login appears.
Symmetric Multiprocessing uses multiple CPUs sharing the same memory. This increases throughput through parallel processing and provides fault tolerance.
The Operating System manages hardware resources, acts as intermediary between applications and hardware, and contains the kernel that stays in memory.
In summary: Know the four components, understand the fetch-decode-execute cycle, memorize the memory hierarchy, and understand interrupts and I/O methods for your exam.
g.text: ✓ Four main components: CPU, Memory, I/O, System Bus
